summary: This notebook demonstrates a rapid inference workflow for a Kaggle Mean Average Precision competition focused on identifying student math misconceptions. It highlights how strategic prompt engineering and a simple popularity-based feature can significantly boost validation scores when fine-tuning large language models. The author provides practical memory-efficient training tips for 1B-parameter models and explains GPU precision constraints for Kaggle's T4 environment. Readers should learn how to adapt tabular text data into effective LLM prompts, implement a custom MAP@3 metric, and streamline the transition from training to quick submission generation.
competition_flow: Loads tabular math question data, engineers a popularity-based correctness feature, formats it into a custom prompt, tokenizes it, trains a 1B-parameter sequence classification model on a 20% holdout, and generates a MAP@3 submission via inference.
data_reading: Reads train and test CSVs with pandas, fills NaN values in the 'Misconception' column with 'NA', creates a combined target string 'Category:Misconception', and applies scikit-learn's LabelEncoder to generate integer labels.
data_processing: Tokenizes text using AutoTokenizer with a maximum length of 256, padding, and truncation, converts DataFrames to Hugging Face Datasets, sets PyTorch tensor formats, and splits data into an 80/20 train-validation holdout.
features_engineering: Derives an 'is_correct' flag by counting answer frequencies per question and assuming the most selected MC_Answer is the ground truth, then merges this feature back into the dataset.
cv_strategy: Random train-test split with 20% holdout validation (test_size=0.2, random_state=42)
insights: Prompt structure directly impacts model performance and validation scores.
Half-precision (fp16) is required for faster inference on Kaggle's T4 GPUs since they lack bf16 support.
Memory-efficient techniques like gradient accumulation, mixed precision, and LoRA enable training of 1B-parameter models on limited VRAM.
critical_findings: Kaggle's T4 GPUs do not support bf16, necessitating fp16 for inference to avoid errors.
The notebook approximates ground-truth answers by assuming the most frequently selected multiple-choice option per question is correct.
